Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 25: LINKING IDENTITY AND POP INFORMATION [Chunk 1] Server policy will determine whether clients are required to use the mechanism specified in this section. This specification provides a method of linking identity and proof-of-possession by including information specific to the current authenticated TLS session within the signed certification request. The client can determine if the server requires the linking of identity and POP by examining the CSR Attributes Response (see Section 4.5.2) [Chunk 2] . Regardless of the CSR Attributes Response, clients SHOULD link identity and POP by embedding tls-unique information in the certification request. If tls-unique information is included by the client, the server MUST verify it. The EST server MAY reject requests without tls-unique information as indicated by server policy. [Chunk 3] Linking identity and proof-of-possession proves to the server that the authenticated TLS client has possession of the private key associated with the certification request, and that the client was able to sign the certification request after the TLS session was established. This is an alternative to the "Linking Identity and POP Information" method defined by Section 6 of [RFC5272] that is available if Full PKI messages are used. [Chunk 4] The client generating the CSR obtains the tls-unique value from the TLS subsystem as described in Channel Bindings for TLS [RFC5929] [Chunk 5] . The EST client operations between obtaining the tls-unique value through generation of the CSR that contains the current tls-unique value and the subsequent verification of this value by the EST server are the "phases of the application protocol during which application- layer authentication occurs"; these operations are protected by the synchronization interoperability mechanism described in the "Channel Bindings for TLS" interoperability notes in Section 3.1 of [RFC5929]. [Chunk 6] When performing renegotiation, TLS "secure_renegotiation" [RFC5746] MUST be used. [Chunk 7] The tls-unique value is base64 encoded as specified in Section 4 of [RFC4648], and the resulting string is placed in the certification request challenge-password field ([RFC2985], Section 5.4.1). The challenge-password field is limited to 255 bytes (Section 7.4.9 of [RFC5246] indicates that no existing cipher suite would result in an issue with this limitation) [Chunk 8] . If the challenge-password attribute is absent, the client did not include the optional channel-binding information (the presence of the challenge-password attribute indicates the inclusion of tls-unique information). [Chunk 9] If the EST server makes use of a back-end infrastructure for processing, it is RECOMMENDED that the results of this verification be communicated. (For example, this communication might use the CMC [RFC5272] "RA POP Witness Control" in a CMC Full PKI Request message. Or, an EST server might TLS-authenticate an EST client as being a trusted infrastructure element that does not forward invalid requests. A detailed discussion of back-end processing is out of scope.) [Chunk 10] When rejecting requests, the EST server response is as described for all enroll responses (Section 4.2.3). If a Full PKI Response is included, the CMCFailInfo MUST be set to popFailed. If a human- readable reject message is included, it SHOULD include an informative text message indicating that the linking of identity and POP information is required.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 25: LINKING IDENTITY AND POP INFORMATION [Chunk 1] Server policy will determine whether clients are required to use the mechanism specified in this section. This specification provides a method of linking identity and proof-of-possession by including information specific to the current authenticated TLS session within the signed certification request. The client can determine if the server requires the linking of identity and POP by examining the CSR Attributes Response (see Section 4.5.2) [Chunk 2] . Regardless of the CSR Attributes Response, clients SHOULD link identity and POP by embedding tls-unique information in the certification request. If tls-unique information is included by the client, the server MUST verify it. The EST server MAY reject requests without tls-unique information as indicated by server policy. [Chunk 3] Linking identity and proof-of-possession proves to the server that the authenticated TLS client has possession of the private key associated with the certification request, and that the client was able to sign the certification request after the TLS session was established. This is an alternative to the "Linking Identity and POP Information" method defined by Section 6 of [RFC5272] that is available if Full PKI messages are used. [Chunk 4] The client generating the CSR obtains the tls-unique value from the TLS subsystem as described in Channel Bindings for TLS [RFC5929] [Chunk 5] . The EST client operations between obtaining the tls-unique value through generation of the CSR that contains the current tls-unique value and the subsequent verification of this value by the EST server are the "phases of the application protocol during which application- layer authentication occurs"; these operations are protected by the synchronization interoperability mechanism described in the "Channel Bindings for TLS" interoperability notes in Section 3.1 of [RFC5929]. [Chunk 6] When performing renegotiation, TLS "secure_renegotiation" [RFC5746] MUST be used. [Chunk 7] The tls-unique value is base64 encoded as specified in Section 4 of [RFC4648], and the resulting string is placed in the certification request challenge-password field ([RFC2985], Section 5.4.1). The challenge-password field is limited to 255 bytes (Section 7.4.9 of [RFC5246] indicates that no existing cipher suite would result in an issue with this limitation) [Chunk 8] . If the challenge-password attribute is absent, the client did not include the optional channel-binding information (the presence of the challenge-password attribute indicates the inclusion of tls-unique information). [Chunk 9] If the EST server makes use of a back-end infrastructure for processing, it is RECOMMENDED that the results of this verification be communicated. (For example, this communication might use the CMC [RFC5272] "RA POP Witness Control" in a CMC Full PKI Request message. Or, an EST server might TLS-authenticate an EST client as being a trusted infrastructure element that does not forward invalid requests. A detailed discussion of back-end processing is out of scope.) [Chunk 10] When rejecting requests, the EST server response is as described for all enroll responses (Section 4.2.3). If a Full PKI Response is included, the CMCFailInfo MUST be set to popFailed. If a human- readable reject message is included, it SHOULD include an informative text message indicating that the linking of identity and POP information is required.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 26: SERVER AUTHORIZATION [Chunk 1] The client MUST check EST server authorization before accepting any server responses or responding to HTTP authentication requests. [Chunk 2] The EST client authorization method depends on which method was used to authenticate the server. When the Explicit TA database is used to authenticate the EST server, then Section 3.6.1 applies. When the Implicit TA database is used to authenticate the EST server, then Section 3.6.2 applies. Successful authentication using a certificate-less cipher suite implies authorization of the server. [Chunk 3] The client MAY perform bootstrapping as specified in Section 4.1.1 even if these checks fail.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 29: CLIENT AUTHORIZATION [Chunk 1] The decision to issue a certificate to a client is always controlled by local CA policy. The EST server configuration reflects this CA policy. This document does not specify any constraints on such policy. EST provides the EST server access to each client's authenticated identity -- e.g., the TLS client's certificate in addition to any HTTP user authentication credentials -- to help in implementing such policy. [Chunk 2] If the client's certificate was issued by the EST CA, and it includes the id-kp-cmcRA [RFC6402] extended key usage extension, then the client is a Registration Authority (RA) as described in [RFC5272] and [RFC6402]. In this case, the EST server SHOULD apply authorization policy consistent with an RA client [Chunk 3] . For example, when handling /simpleenroll requests, the EST server could be configured to accept POP linking information that does not match the current TLS session because the authenticated EST client RA has verified this information when acting as an EST server (as specified in Section 3.5). More specific RA mechanisms are available if the EST client uses /fullcmc methods.

EST provides the EST server access to each client's authenticated identity -- e.g., the TLS client's certificate in addition to any HTTP user authentication credentials -- to help in implementing such policy. [Chunk 2] If the client's certificate was issued by the EST CA, and it includes the id-kp-cmcRA [RFC6402] extended key usage extension, then the client is a Registration Authority (RA) as described in [RFC5272] and [RFC6402]. In this case, the EST server SHOULD apply authorization policy consistent with an RA client [Chunk 3] . For example, when handling /simpleenroll requests, the EST server could be configured to accept POP linking information that does not match the current TLS session because the authenticated EST client RA has verified this information when acting as an EST server (as specified in Section 3.5). More specific RA mechanisms are available if the EST client uses /fullcmc methods.
Reason: This test configures client authentication using a client private key (client certificate mode) and asserts est_client_set_auth succeeded. It exercises the client-certificate authentication configuration and that the client API accepts key-based auth input; further calls (get_cacerts) follow, demonstrating usage in a client context. This verifies client-side certificate auth setup; end-to-end acceptance by server is implied by subsequent requests but not fully detailed here. | Duplicate/alternate test chunk also verifying client certificate-based auth configuration succeeded via est_client_set_auth; provides additional evidence for client cert auth configuration being exercised. | Chunk contains utility code (cert verification behavior) and a descriptive comment about a test case; no direct test assertions for client authorization are present in this snippet. | This test shows a client setting a private key (client TLS identity) via est_client_set_auth, which exercises the client-side capability to present a TLS client certificate/private key. It does not contain any server-side assertion that the server received or used the client's identity, so it only partially supports the requirement (client can present identity). | This test starts an EST server using a specific CA cert chain (including CRLs) argument (US897_CACERTS_MULTI_CHAIN_CRLS) and verifies startup succeeded and that CA certs are readable. That demonstrates the server can be configured with CA trust material (reflecting CA policy). It does not, however, assert that the server uses client identity during issuance decisions. | Similar to chunk61, this test starts the server with a designated CA cert chain (US897_CACERTS_SINGLE_CHAIN_MULT_CERTS) and verifies CA certs are present, supporting the server-configures-CA-policy aspect; it does not validate server-side access to client identity for issuance decisions. | Client sets TLS auth and successfully retrieves CA certs from server; demonstrates client identity can be supplied and server returns CA certs, but no server-side assertion of identity exposure for policy enforcement. | This chunk tests read-timeout parameter validation; unrelated to CA policy configuration or client identity exposure. | Chunk contains client init/set_auth/get_cacerts calls and CU_ASSERT on return codes but does not reference id-kp-cmcRA, RA classification, or server authorization policy. | Initialization test with CU_ASSERT; no assertions or setup related to id-kp-cmcRA or RA behavior. | Sets authentication and asserts success; no evidence of testing id-kp-cmcRA detection or RA-specific authorization. | Contains est_client_set_auth and cleanup assertions but no RA/ id-kp-cmcRA related checks. | Tests parameter validation for set_auth; unrelated to id-kp-cmcRA detection or RA policy.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 32: BOOTSTRAP DISTRIBUTION OF CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] It is possible that the client was not configured with an Implicit TA database that allows a bootstrap installation of the Explicit TA database as described in 4.1.3. This section describes an alternate method by which minimally configured EST clients can populate their Explicit TA database. [Chunk 2] If the EST client application does not specify either an Explicit TA database or an Implicit TA database, then the initial TLS server authentication and authorization will fail. The client MAY provisionally continue the TLS handshake to completion for the purposes of accessing the /cacerts or /fullcmc method [Chunk 3] . If the EST client continues with an unauthenticated connection, the client MUST extract the HTTP content data from the response (Sections 4.1.3 or 4.3.2) and engage a human user to authorize the CA certificate using out-of-band data such as a CA certificate "fingerprint" (e.g., a SHA-256 or SHA-512 [SHS] hash on the whole CA certificate). In a /fullcmc response, it is the Publish Trust Anchors control (CMC [RFC5272], Section 6.15) within the Full PKI Response that must be accepted manually [Chunk 4] . It is incumbent on the user to properly verify the TA information, or to provide the "fingerprint" data during configuration that is necessary to verify the TA information. [Chunk 5] HTTP authentication requests MUST NOT be responded to if the server has not been authenticated as specified in Section 3.3.1 or if the optional certificate-less authentication is used as specified in Section 3.3.3. [Chunk 6] The EST client uses the /cacerts response to establish an Explicit Trust Anchor database for subsequent TLS authentication of the EST server. EST clients MUST NOT engage in any other protocol exchange until after the /cacerts response has been accepted and a new TLS session has been established (using TLS certificate-based authentication).

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 32: BOOTSTRAP DISTRIBUTION OF CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] It is possible that the client was not configured with an Implicit TA database that allows a bootstrap installation of the Explicit TA database as described in 4.1.3. This section describes an alternate method by which minimally configured EST clients can populate their Explicit TA database. [Chunk 2] If the EST client application does not specify either an Explicit TA database or an Implicit TA database, then the initial TLS server authentication and authorization will fail. The client MAY provisionally continue the TLS handshake to completion for the purposes of accessing the /cacerts or /fullcmc method [Chunk 3] . If the EST client continues with an unauthenticated connection, the client MUST extract the HTTP content data from the response (Sections 4.1.3 or 4.3.2) and engage a human user to authorize the CA certificate using out-of-band data such as a CA certificate "fingerprint" (e.g., a SHA-256 or SHA-512 [SHS] hash on the whole CA certificate). In a /fullcmc response, it is the Publish Trust Anchors control (CMC [RFC5272], Section 6.15) within the Full PKI Response that must be accepted manually [Chunk 4] . It is incumbent on the user to properly verify the TA information, or to provide the "fingerprint" data during configuration that is necessary to verify the TA information. [Chunk 5] HTTP authentication requests MUST NOT be responded to if the server has not been authenticated as specified in Section 3.3.1 or if the optional certificate-less authentication is used as specified in Section 3.3.3. [Chunk 6] The EST client uses the /cacerts response to establish an Explicit Trust Anchor database for subsequent TLS authentication of the EST server. EST clients MUST NOT engage in any other protocol exchange until after the /cacerts response has been accepted and a new TLS session has been established (using TLS certificate-based authentication).
Reason: File-scope macro definitions for cert paths; no assertions to validate distribution. | Happy-path enrollment with provided credentials; not about missing trust anchors or provisional /cacerts retrieval. | Shows successful enroll and copy operations; does not exercise failure-on-missing-TA or provisional /cacerts behavior. | Duplicate happy-path enrollment scenario; not relevant to missing TA DB behavior. | Initialization and successful enroll; not exercising absent-trust-anchor behavior. | Sets auth, server, and performs enroll_csr assertions. No evidence of extracting CA cert HTTP payload when unauthenticated or prompting human for fingerprint verification. | Changes sign digest, sets auth, and expects a BAD_REQ result for enroll_csr; no relationship to extracting CA cert content for unauthenticated connections or manual fingerprint-based acceptance. | Copies enrolled cert and cleans up; test contains no assertions about extracting HTTP payloads for unauthenticated connections nor prompting a user to accept trust anchors via fingerprint. | Performs enroll_csr and cert copy on success; lacks assertions about unauthenticated HTTP content handling or human OOB fingerprint acceptance. | Duplicate enroll/copy flow similar to other chunks; no tests for extracting CA cert HTTP payloads or human fingerprint authorization. | Defines constants and extern; no evidence of TA/fingerprint configuration or verification. | This chunk sets auth, server, and performs enroll; no evidence of /cacerts retrieval or enforcement of sequencing before other protocol exchanges. | Creates client and performs enroll_csr; not exercising /cacerts retrieval or sequencing requirement. | Defines constants and externs; no runtime behavior covering /cacerts handling. | Enroll and copy enrolled cert; does not demonstrate retrieval of /cacerts or enforcement of sequencing prior to other exchanges. | Duplicate enroll/copy flow; no /cacerts retrieval or sequencing assertions.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 32: BOOTSTRAP DISTRIBUTION OF CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] It is possible that the client was not configured with an Implicit TA database that allows a bootstrap installation of the Explicit TA database as described in 4.1.3. This section describes an alternate method by which minimally configured EST clients can populate their Explicit TA database. [Chunk 2] If the EST client application does not specify either an Explicit TA database or an Implicit TA database, then the initial TLS server authentication and authorization will fail. The client MAY provisionally continue the TLS handshake to completion for the purposes of accessing the /cacerts or /fullcmc method [Chunk 3] . If the EST client continues with an unauthenticated connection, the client MUST extract the HTTP content data from the response (Sections 4.1.3 or 4.3.2) and engage a human user to authorize the CA certificate using out-of-band data such as a CA certificate "fingerprint" (e.g., a SHA-256 or SHA-512 [SHS] hash on the whole CA certificate). In a /fullcmc response, it is the Publish Trust Anchors control (CMC [RFC5272], Section 6.15) within the Full PKI Response that must be accepted manually [Chunk 4] . It is incumbent on the user to properly verify the TA information, or to provide the "fingerprint" data during configuration that is necessary to verify the TA information. [Chunk 5] HTTP authentication requests MUST NOT be responded to if the server has not been authenticated as specified in Section 3.3.1 or if the optional certificate-less authentication is used as specified in Section 3.3.3. [Chunk 6] The EST client uses the /cacerts response to establish an Explicit Trust Anchor database for subsequent TLS authentication of the EST server. EST clients MUST NOT engage in any other protocol exchange until after the /cacerts response has been accepted and a new TLS session has been established (using TLS certificate-based authentication).

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 34: CA CERTIFICATES RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code. Any other response code indicates an error and the client MUST abort the protocol. [Chunk 2] A successful response MUST be a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response, as defined in [RFC5272], containing the certificates described in the following paragraph. The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" is used. The Simple PKI Response is sent with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 3] The EST server MUST include the current root CA certificate in the response. The EST server MUST include any additional certificates the client would need to build a chain from an EST CA-issued certificate to the current EST CA TA. For example, if the EST CA is a subordinate CA, then all the appropriate subordinate CA certificates necessary to build a chain to the root EST CA are included in the response. [Chunk 4] The EST server SHOULD include the three "Root CA Key Update" certificates OldWithOld, OldWithNew, and NewWithOld in the response chain. These are defined in Section 4.4 of CMP [RFC4210]. The EST client MUST be able to handle these certificates in the response. The EST CA's most recent self-signed certificate (e.g., NewWithNew certificate) is self-signed and has the latest NotAfter date [Chunk 5] . If the EST server does not include these in the response, then after the current EST CA certificate expires, the EST clients will need to be reinitialized with the PKI using the Bootstrap Distribution of CA certificates (Section 4.1.1) method, which involves user interaction. [Chunk 6] After out-of-band validation occurs, all the other certificates MUST be validated using normal [RFC5280] certificate path validation (using the most recent CA certificate as the TA) before they can be used to build certificate paths during certificate validation. [Chunk 7] The EST client MUST store the extracted EST CA certificate as an Explicit TA database entry for subsequent EST server authentication. The EST client SHOULD disable use of Implicit TA database entries for this EST server now that an Explicit TA database entry is available [Chunk 8] . If the client disables the Implicit TA database, and if the EST server certificate was verified using an Implicit TA database entry, then the client MUST include the "Trusted CA Indication" extension in future TLS sessions [RFC6066]. This indicates to the server that only an EST server certificate authenticatable by the Explicit TA database entry is now acceptable (otherwise, the EST server might continue to use a server certificate that is only verifiable by a now disabled Implicit TA). [Chunk 9] The EST client SHOULD also make the CA Certificate response information available to the end-entity software for use when validating peer certificates.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 34: CA CERTIFICATES RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code. Any other response code indicates an error and the client MUST abort the protocol. [Chunk 2] A successful response MUST be a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response, as defined in [RFC5272], containing the certificates described in the following paragraph. The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" is used. The Simple PKI Response is sent with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 3] The EST server MUST include the current root CA certificate in the response. The EST server MUST include any additional certificates the client would need to build a chain from an EST CA-issued certificate to the current EST CA TA. For example, if the EST CA is a subordinate CA, then all the appropriate subordinate CA certificates necessary to build a chain to the root EST CA are included in the response. [Chunk 4] The EST server SHOULD include the three "Root CA Key Update" certificates OldWithOld, OldWithNew, and NewWithOld in the response chain. These are defined in Section 4.4 of CMP [RFC4210]. The EST client MUST be able to handle these certificates in the response. The EST CA's most recent self-signed certificate (e.g., NewWithNew certificate) is self-signed and has the latest NotAfter date [Chunk 5] . If the EST server does not include these in the response, then after the current EST CA certificate expires, the EST clients will need to be reinitialized with the PKI using the Bootstrap Distribution of CA certificates (Section 4.1.1) method, which involves user interaction. [Chunk 6] After out-of-band validation occurs, all the other certificates MUST be validated using normal [RFC5280] certificate path validation (using the most recent CA certificate as the TA) before they can be used to build certificate paths during certificate validation. [Chunk 7] The EST client MUST store the extracted EST CA certificate as an Explicit TA database entry for subsequent EST server authentication. The EST client SHOULD disable use of Implicit TA database entries for this EST server now that an Explicit TA database entry is available [Chunk 8] . If the client disables the Implicit TA database, and if the EST server certificate was verified using an Implicit TA database entry, then the client MUST include the "Trusted CA Indication" extension in future TLS sessions [RFC6066]. This indicates to the server that only an EST server certificate authenticatable by the Explicit TA database entry is now acceptable (otherwise, the EST server might continue to use a server certificate that is only verifiable by a now disabled Implicit TA). [Chunk 9] The EST client SHOULD also make the CA Certificate response information available to the end-entity software for use when validating peer certificates.

This is strong evidence the data is base64-encoded PKCS7 with certificates, but it does not validate the HTTP Content-Type header or prove the CMC 'certs-only Simple PKI' envelope semantics required by the requirement. | Enrolled cert retrieval and subsequent client init steps; not directly asserting /cacerts content. | Test executes est_client_reenroll and cleanup. Does not contain assertions linking missing server-provided CA certs to client reinitialization or user interaction. | Retrieves enrolled cert and reinitializes client context for token mode; does not assert or test that absence of server-provided CA certs triggers client reinitialization via bootstrap/user interaction after CA expiry. | Initializes client context, sets auth callback and enrolls. Does not assert behavior about missing CA certs in responses or the requirement to reinitialize clients via bootstrap after CA expiry. | As with similar pkcs7 checks, this ensures a cert is present but does not assert RFC5280 path validation using a recent TA prior to usage. | Test copies an enrolled cert and then re-initializes a client context using a cacerts buffer. This demonstrates use of an explicit CA set for a new client context but does not show that the library automatically persisted a retrieved EST CA cert as an Explicit TA entry or that implicit TAs were disabled for that server. The linkage from retrieval to automatic storage/disabling is not asserted. | Client init and enroll assertions; absence of checks for Trusted CA Indication extension or TA DB disable/transition. | Simple enroll helper; no checks for Trusted CA Indication extension. | Switches server to token mode and re-inits client; lacks checks for Trusted CA Indication extension when implicit TA DB is disabled. | This chunk retrieves an enrolled cert and reinitializes a client context then sets auth callbacks; it does not exercise retrieval or provision of CA cert response for end-entity validation. | Initializes client and performs enrollment; no explicit retrieval or copying of CA cert response for use by the end-entity is present in this snippet.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 34: CA CERTIFICATES RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code. Any other response code indicates an error and the client MUST abort the protocol. [Chunk 2] A successful response MUST be a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response, as defined in [RFC5272], containing the certificates described in the following paragraph. The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" is used. The Simple PKI Response is sent with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 3] The EST server MUST include the current root CA certificate in the response. The EST server MUST include any additional certificates the client would need to build a chain from an EST CA-issued certificate to the current EST CA TA. For example, if the EST CA is a subordinate CA, then all the appropriate subordinate CA certificates necessary to build a chain to the root EST CA are included in the response. [Chunk 4] The EST server SHOULD include the three "Root CA Key Update" certificates OldWithOld, OldWithNew, and NewWithOld in the response chain. These are defined in Section 4.4 of CMP [RFC4210]. The EST client MUST be able to handle these certificates in the response. The EST CA's most recent self-signed certificate (e.g., NewWithNew certificate) is self-signed and has the latest NotAfter date [Chunk 5] . If the EST server does not include these in the response, then after the current EST CA certificate expires, the EST clients will need to be reinitialized with the PKI using the Bootstrap Distribution of CA certificates (Section 4.1.1) method, which involves user interaction. [Chunk 6] After out-of-band validation occurs, all the other certificates MUST be validated using normal [RFC5280] certificate path validation (using the most recent CA certificate as the TA) before they can be used to build certificate paths during certificate validation. [Chunk 7] The EST client MUST store the extracted EST CA certificate as an Explicit TA database entry for subsequent EST server authentication. The EST client SHOULD disable use of Implicit TA database entries for this EST server now that an Explicit TA database entry is available [Chunk 8] . If the client disables the Implicit TA database, and if the EST server certificate was verified using an Implicit TA database entry, then the client MUST include the "Trusted CA Indication" extension in future TLS sessions [RFC6066]. This indicates to the server that only an EST server certificate authenticatable by the Explicit TA database entry is now acceptable (otherwise, the EST server might continue to use a server certificate that is only verifiable by a now disabled Implicit TA). [Chunk 9] The EST client SHOULD also make the CA Certificate response information available to the end-entity software for use when validating peer certificates.

(Section 4.2.3 describes the use of an HTTP 202 response to the EST client if this occurs.)

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 36: SIMPLE ENROLLMENT OF CLIENTS [Chunk 1] When HTTPS POSTing to /simpleenroll, the client MUST include a Simple PKI Request as specified in CMC [RFC5272], Section 3.1 (i.e., a PKCS https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7030#10 Certification Request [RFC2986]). [Chunk 2] The Certification Signing Request (CSR) signature provides proof-of-possession of the client-possessed private key to the EST server. If the CSR KeyUsage extension indicates that the private key can be used to generate digital signatures, then the client MUST generate the CSR signature using the private key [Chunk 3] . If the key can be used to generate digital signatures but the requested CSR KeyUsage extension prohibits generation of digital signatures, then the CSR signature MAY still be generated using the private key, but the key MUST NOT be used for any other signature operations (this is consistent with the recommendations concerning submission of proof-of-possession to an RA or CA as described in [SP-800-57-Part-1]) [Chunk 4] . The use of /fullcmc operations provides access to more advanced proof-of-possession methods that are used when the key pair cannot be used for digital signature generation (see Section 4.3). [Chunk 5] The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs10" is used here. The format of the message is as specified in [RFC5967] with a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 6] If the EST client authenticated using a previously installed certificate issued by a third-party CA (see Section 2.2.1), the client MAY include the ChangeSubjectName attribute, as defined in [RFC6402], in the CSR to request that the subjectName and SubjectAltName be changed in the new certificate. [Chunk 7] The EST client MAY request additional certificates even when using an existing certificate in the TLS client authentication. For example, the client can use an existing certificate for TLS client authentication when requesting a certificate that cannot be used for TLS client authentication.

It does not assert or exercise '/fullcmc' or advanced PoP methods for non-signing keypairs. | Performs client init, sets auth, and generates a key; it does not reference '/fullcmc' or alternate PoP for non-signing keys. | Helper for easy provision; no explicit '/fullcmc' or advanced PoP testing. | Reads client key and cert into test; no references to '/fullcmc' or alternative PoP mechanisms for non-signing keys. | Contains negative tests for est_client_provision_cert parameter validation. This verifies parameter error handling but does not exercise '/fullcmc' or advanced PoP methods for non-signing keypairs. | Copies enrolled certificate and CA certs from context and asserts success; no assertions inspect subjectName or SubjectAltName for changes requested via ChangeSubjectName. | Copies enrolled cert and CA certs from the context; chunk itself contains retrieval logic but not the initial configuration lines that show using an existing TLS client certificate while requesting an additional certificate (those are in a different chunk). | The test explicitly calls est_client_set_auth with a non-NULL client certificate/key (cert, key) to identify the client (implying TLS client certificate authentication) and then calls est_client_provision_cert with a separate new_key to request/provision a new certificate. The test asserts success (CU_ASSERT(rv == EST_ERR_NONE)), directly demonstrating the client requesting an additional certificate while using an existing certificate for client authentication.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 36: SIMPLE ENROLLMENT OF CLIENTS [Chunk 1] When HTTPS POSTing to /simpleenroll, the client MUST include a Simple PKI Request as specified in CMC [RFC5272], Section 3.1 (i.e., a PKCS https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7030#10 Certification Request [RFC2986]). [Chunk 2] The Certification Signing Request (CSR) signature provides proof-of-possession of the client-possessed private key to the EST server. If the CSR KeyUsage extension indicates that the private key can be used to generate digital signatures, then the client MUST generate the CSR signature using the private key [Chunk 3] . If the key can be used to generate digital signatures but the requested CSR KeyUsage extension prohibits generation of digital signatures, then the CSR signature MAY still be generated using the private key, but the key MUST NOT be used for any other signature operations (this is consistent with the recommendations concerning submission of proof-of-possession to an RA or CA as described in [SP-800-57-Part-1]) [Chunk 4] . The use of /fullcmc operations provides access to more advanced proof-of-possession methods that are used when the key pair cannot be used for digital signature generation (see Section 4.3). [Chunk 5] The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs10" is used here. The format of the message is as specified in [RFC5967] with a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 6] If the EST client authenticated using a previously installed certificate issued by a third-party CA (see Section 2.2.1), the client MAY include the ChangeSubjectName attribute, as defined in [RFC6402], in the CSR to request that the subjectName and SubjectAltName be changed in the new certificate. [Chunk 7] The EST client MAY request additional certificates even when using an existing certificate in the TLS client authentication. For example, the client can use an existing certificate for TLS client authentication when requesting a certificate that cannot be used for TLS client authentication.

No assertions enforce prohibiting other signature uses of the key. | Comment describing CSR generation command; no runtime assertions or enroll/sign invocation. | Comment describing CSR creation via openssl; no assertions or sign/enroll operations in this chunk. | This chunk defines generate_private_key(); no enroll/sign assertions are present here. | Macro definition only; no runtime verification of CSR signing policy. | Same stale PoP CSR constant as other files. Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 36: SIMPLE ENROLLMENT OF CLIENTS [Chunk 1] When HTTPS POSTing to /simpleenroll, the client MUST include a Simple PKI Request as specified in CMC [RFC5272], Section 3.1 (i.e., a PKCS https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7030#10 Certification Request [RFC2986]). [Chunk 6] If the EST client authenticated using a previously installed certificate issued by a third-party CA (see Section 2.2.1), the client MAY include the ChangeSubjectName attribute, as defined in [RFC6402], in the CSR to request that the subjectName and SubjectAltName be changed in the new certificate. [Chunk 7] The EST client MAY request additional certificates even when using an existing certificate in the TLS client authentication. For example, the client can use an existing certificate for TLS client authentication when requesting a certificate that cannot be used for TLS client authentication.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 37: SIMPLE RE-ENROLLMENT OF CLIENTS [Chunk 1] EST clients renew/rekey certificates with an HTTPS POST using the operation path value of "/simplereenroll". [Chunk 2] A certificate request employs the same format as the "simpleenroll" request, using the same HTTP content-type. The request Subject field and SubjectAltName extension MUST be identical to the corresponding fields in the certificate being renewed/rekeyed. The ChangeSubjectName attribute, as defined in [RFC6402], MAY be included in the CSR to request that these fields be changed in the new certificate. [Chunk 3] If the Subject Public Key Info in the certification request is the same as the current client certificate, then the EST server renews the client certificate. The request Subject field and SubjectAltName extension MUST be identical to the corresponding fields in the certificate being renewed/rekeyed. The ChangeSubjectName attribute, as defined in [RFC6402], MAY be included in the CSR to request that these fields be changed in the new certificate. [Chunk 3] If the Subject Public Key Info in the certification request is the same as the current client certificate, then the EST server renews the client certificate. If the public key information in the certification request is different than the current client certificate, then the EST server rekeys the client certificate.
Reason: Defines ENROLL/CACERT URLs for simple enroll; no re-enroll evidence in this chunk. | Defines content-type and URLs; no test assertions shown verifying subject/SAN equality or ChangeSubjectName handling.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 38: SIMPLE ENROLL AND RE-ENROLL RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If the enrollment is successful, the server response MUST contain an HTTP 200 response code with a content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime". [Chunk 2] A successful response MUST be a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response, as defined in [RFC5272], containing only the certificate that was issued. The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" with an smime-type parameter "certs-only" is used, as specified in [RFC5273]. [Chunk 3] The server MUST answer with a suitable 4xx or 5xx HTTP [RFC2616] error code when a problem occurs. A Simple PKI Response with an HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" (see Section 4.3.2) MAY be included in the response data to convey an error response. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human- readable error message containing explanatory information describing why the request was rejected (for example, indicating that CSR attributes are incomplete). [Chunk 4] If the server responds with an HTTP [RFC2616] 202, this indicates that the request has been accepted for processing but that a response is not yet available. The server MUST include a Retry-After header as defined for HTTP 503 responses. The server also MAY include informative human-readable content. The client MUST wait at least the specified "retry-after" time before repeating the same request [Chunk 5] . The client repeats the initial enrollment request after the appropriate "retry-after" interval has expired. The client SHOULD log or inform the end-user of this event. The server is responsible for maintaining all states necessary to recognize and handle retry operations as the client is stateless in this regard; it simply sends the same request repeatedly until it receives a different response code. All other return codes are handled as specified in HTTP [RFC2616]. [Chunk 6] If the client closes the TLS connections while waiting for the Retry- After time to expire, then the client initiates a new TLS connection and performs all applicable security checks. If the client has already generated a CSR that includes linking identity and POP information (Section 3.5), then the CSR will need to be recreated to incorporate the tls-unique from the new, redirected session. Note: the key pair need not be regenerated. These are processing and interface burdens on the client [Chunk 7] . EST server administrators are advised to take this into consideration. [Chunk 8] The EST client MAY also make the certificate response, and associated private key, available to end-entity software for use as an end-entity certificate.
Reason: Comment about server configuration; no runtime assertions related to the requirement. | Comment about server configuration; not testing the required client behavior. | Provisioning API is invoked successfully and returns a pkcs7_len, which indicates a certificate response was obtained/stored. However, test does not show copying certificate into an application buffer nor any mechanism to obtain a private key from the library; the new_key here is an application-generated key passed in, not returned.

[Chunk 2] A successful response MUST be a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response, as defined in [RFC5272], containing only the certificate that was issued. The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" with an smime-type parameter "certs-only" is used, as specified in [RFC5273]. [Chunk 3] The server MUST answer with a suitable 4xx or 5xx HTTP [RFC2616] error code when a problem occurs. A Simple PKI Response with an HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" (see Section 4.3.2) MAY be included in the response data to convey an error response. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human- readable error message containing explanatory information describing why the request was rejected (for example, indicating that CSR attributes are incomplete). [Chunk 4] If the server responds with an HTTP [RFC2616] 202, this indicates that the request has been accepted for processing but that a response is not yet available. The server MUST include a Retry-After header as defined for HTTP 503 responses. The server also MAY include informative human-readable content. The client MUST wait at least the specified "retry-after" time before repeating the same request [Chunk 5] . The client repeats the initial enrollment request after the appropriate "retry-after" interval has expired. The client SHOULD log or inform the end-user of this event. The server is responsible for maintaining all states necessary to recognize and handle retry operations as the client is stateless in this regard; it simply sends the same request repeatedly until it receives a different response code. All other return codes are handled as specified in HTTP [RFC2616]. [Chunk 6] If the client closes the TLS connections while waiting for the Retry- After time to expire, then the client initiates a new TLS connection and performs all applicable security checks. If the client has already generated a CSR that includes linking identity and POP information (Section 3.5), then the CSR will need to be recreated to incorporate the tls-unique from the new, redirected session. Note: the key pair need not be regenerated. These are processing and interface burdens on the client [Chunk 7] . EST server administrators are advised to take this into consideration. [Chunk 8] The EST client MAY also make the certificate response, and associated private key, available to end-entity software for use as an end-entity certificate.
Reason: Comment describes re-enroll path-segment behavior to be verified but no actual assertions or test code are present in the provided snippet. | Defines request Content-Type for PKCS#10 (application/pkcs10) in test harness; does not assert or verify the response Content-Type of application/pkcs7-mime or HTTP 200 in the provided snippet. | This test asserts successful retrieval of CSR attributes (csrattrs) and path handling. It does not verify that an enrollment response is a certs-only CMC Simple PKI Response or that the Content-Type is application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=certs-only. | Macro only; no assertions relevant to response content or headers. | Defines enroll URLs and request content-type for PKCS10; no assertions about response Content-Type or certs-only payload. | Asserts that the first certificate in a parsed cert stack is present. This is partial evidence that a returned PKCS7 contains a certificate but does not demonstrate that only that certificate was returned nor does it verify HTTP Content-Type or CMC wrapping. | Commentary describing intent; no assertions verifying retry-after handling or automatic retry. | This test calls est_client_reenroll and asserts success. Reenroll invokes client TLS connect and the code path that removes existing PoP and will add a tls-unique if required. However, the test does not explicitly assert that the CSR was recreated with a new tls-unique nor that the TLS handshake used new security checks after a closed-wait period. Thus partial coverage: reenroll path exercised but not the explicit CSR recreation verification for tls-unique after closing during Retry-After. | Enroll succeeded (pkcs7_len returned) but no explicit est_client_copy_enrolled_cert() call in this chunk. No evidence of private-key retrieval. | Test invokes est_client_enroll and asserts an expected result. If expected_enroll_rv corresponds to success, this implies an enroll response was produced; however, no explicit copy of the enrolled cert is shown and there is no evidence of returning an associated private key.

The HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" with an smime-type parameter "certs-only" is used, as specified in [RFC5273]. [Chunk 3] The server MUST answer with a suitable 4xx or 5xx HTTP [RFC2616] error code when a problem occurs. A Simple PKI Response with an HTTP content-type of "application/pkcs7-mime" (see Section 4.3.2) MAY be included in the response data to convey an error response. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human- readable error message containing explanatory information describing why the request was rejected (for example, indicating that CSR attributes are incomplete). [Chunk 4] If the server responds with an HTTP [RFC2616] 202, this indicates that the request has been accepted for processing but that a response is not yet available. The server MUST include a Retry-After header as defined for HTTP 503 responses. The server also MAY include informative human-readable content. The client MUST wait at least the specified "retry-after" time before repeating the same request [Chunk 5] . The client repeats the initial enrollment request after the appropriate "retry-after" interval has expired. The client SHOULD log or inform the end-user of this event. The server is responsible for maintaining all states necessary to recognize and handle retry operations as the client is stateless in this regard; it simply sends the same request repeatedly until it receives a different response code. All other return codes are handled as specified in HTTP [RFC2616]. [Chunk 6] If the client closes the TLS connections while waiting for the Retry- After time to expire, then the client initiates a new TLS connection and performs all applicable security checks. If the client has already generated a CSR that includes linking identity and POP information (Section 3.5), then the CSR will need to be recreated to incorporate the tls-unique from the new, redirected session. Note: the key pair need not be regenerated. These are processing and interface burdens on the client [Chunk 7] . EST server administrators are advised to take this into consideration. [Chunk 8] The EST client MAY also make the certificate response, and associated private key, available to end-entity software for use as an end-entity certificate.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 4: OBTAINING CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] The EST client can request a copy of the current EST CA certificate(s) from the EST server. The EST client is assumed to perform this operation before performing other operations. [Chunk 2] Throughout this document we assume the EST CA has a certificate that is used by the client to verify signed objects issued by the CA, e.g., certificates and certificate revocation lists (CRLs), and that a different certificate than the one used to verify signatures on certificates and CRLs is used when EST protocol communication requires additional encryption. [Chunk 3] The EST client authenticates and verifies the authorization scope of the EST server when requesting the current CA certificate(s). As detailed in Sections 3.3.1 and 3.3.3, available options include: [Chunk 4] Verifying the EST server's HTTPS URI against the EST server's certificate using Implicit TAs (similar to a common HTTPS exchange). This allows the EST server and client to leverage existing TAs that might be known to the EST client. [Chunk 5] The client can leverage a previously distributed trust anchor specific to the EST server. This allows the EST client to use an existing, potentially older, CA certificate to request a current CA certificate. [Chunk 6] For bootstrapping, the EST client can rely upon manual authentication performed by the end-user as detailed in Section 4.1.1. [Chunk 7] The client can leverage the binding of a shared credential to a specific EST server with a certificate-less TLS cipher suite. Client authentication is not required for this exchange, so it is trivially supported by the EST server.

No assertions relate to the use of a separate encryption certificate (part B). | This test asserts that copying retrieved CA certs from the client context succeeds (EST_ERR_NONE), which is weak evidence that CA certs had been retrieved earlier and stored; it does not explicitly assert server authentication or authorization-scope checks. | est_client_provision_cert (per source) calls est_client_get_cacerts() internally. The successful assertion indicates the combined flow (including retrieval of CA certs) completed; however the test does not assert explicit server authorization-scope verification behavior or negative/error cases (e.g. FQDN mismatch or cacert verification failure). | Copies enrolled cert and CACerts out of context; no explicit verification assertions demonstrating implicit-TA hostname verification. | This snippet retrieves the enrolled certificate and then explicitly retrieves a copy of the new CA certs via est_client_copy_cacerts(ectx,...), asserting success. This demonstrates a client obtaining current CA certs from an EST context. It is partial because the snippet does not explicitly show the client being initialized with a previously-distributed (older) trust anchor prior to making the request, though such initialization is likely present elsewhere in the test harness. | Uses est_client_set_auth with provided cert/key and static credentials; does not show manual authentication callback usage. | This test uses est_client_set_auth with a client certificate and private key (cert, key provided), so it does not demonstrate certificate-less TLS cipher suite or absence of client certificate authentication.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 4: OBTAINING CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] The EST client can request a copy of the current EST CA certificate(s) from the EST server. The EST client is assumed to perform this operation before performing other operations. [Chunk 2] Throughout this document we assume the EST CA has a certificate that is used by the client to verify signed objects issued by the CA, e.g., certificates and certificate revocation lists (CRLs), and that a different certificate than the one used to verify signatures on certificates and CRLs is used when EST protocol communication requires additional encryption. [Chunk 3] The EST client authenticates and verifies the authorization scope of the EST server when requesting the current CA certificate(s). As detailed in Sections 3.3.1 and 3.3.3, available options include: [Chunk 4] Verifying the EST server's HTTPS URI against the EST server's certificate using Implicit TAs (similar to a common HTTPS exchange). This allows the EST server and client to leverage existing TAs that might be known to the EST client. [Chunk 5] The client can leverage a previously distributed trust anchor specific to the EST server. This allows the EST client to use an existing, potentially older, CA certificate to request a current CA certificate. [Chunk 6] For bootstrapping, the EST client can rely upon manual authentication performed by the end-user as detailed in Section 4.1.1. [Chunk 7] The client can leverage the binding of a shared credential to a specific EST server with a certificate-less TLS cipher suite. Client authentication is not required for this exchange, so it is trivially supported by the EST server.
Reason: This test reads local CA certs and initializes client context with them and asserts successful auth setup — covering local-provision happy path for obtaining CA certs by the client.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 4: OBTAINING CA CERTIFICATES [Chunk 1] The EST client can request a copy of the current EST CA certificate(s) from the EST server. The EST client is assumed to perform this operation before performing other operations. [Chunk 2] Throughout this document we assume the EST CA has a certificate that is used by the client to verify signed objects issued by the CA, e.g., certificates and certificate revocation lists (CRLs), and that a different certificate than the one used to verify signatures on certificates and CRLs is used when EST protocol communication requires additional encryption. [Chunk 3] The EST client authenticates and verifies the authorization scope of the EST server when requesting the current CA certificate(s). As detailed in Sections 3.3.1 and 3.3.3, available options include: [Chunk 4] Verifying the EST server's HTTPS URI against the EST server's certificate using Implicit TAs (similar to a common HTTPS exchange). This allows the EST server and client to leverage existing TAs that might be known to the EST client. [Chunk 5] The client can leverage a previously distributed trust anchor specific to the EST server. This allows the EST client to use an existing, potentially older, CA certificate to request a current CA certificate. [Chunk 6] For bootstrapping, the EST client can rely upon manual authentication performed by the end-user as detailed in Section 4.1.1. [Chunk 7] The client can leverage the binding of a shared credential to a specific EST server with a certificate-less TLS cipher suite. Client authentication is not required for this exchange, so it is trivially supported by the EST server.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 42: SERVER-SIDE KEY GENERATION [Chunk 1] An EST client may request a private key and associated certificate from an EST server using an HTTPS POST with an operation path value of "/serverkeygen". Support for the /serverkeygen function is OPTIONAL. [Chunk 2] A client MUST authenticate an EST server, as specified in Section 3.3.1 if certificate-based authentication is used or Section 3.3.3 if the optional certificate-less authentication is used, and check the server's authorization as given in Section 3.6. [Chunk 3] The EST server MUST authenticate the client, as specified in Section 3.3.2 if certificate-based authenticated is used or Section 3.3.3 if the optional certificate-less authentication is used, and check the client's authorization as given in Section 3.7. The EST server applies whatever authorization or logic it chooses to determine if the private key and certificate should be provided. [Chunk 4] Cipher suites that have a NULL confidentiality algorithm MUST NOT be used as they will disclose the contents of an unprotected private key. [Chunk 5] Proper random number and key generation [RFC4086] is a server implementation responsibility, and server archiving of generated keys is determined by CA policy. The key pair and certificate are transferred over the TLS session. The cipher suite used to return the private key and certificate MUST offer confidentiality commensurate with the private key being delivered to the client. [Chunk 6] The EST client MAY request additional certificates even when using an existing certificate in the TLS client authentication. For example, the client can use an existing certificate for TLS client authentication when requesting a certificate that cannot be used for TLS client authentication.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 45: REQUESTS FOR ASYMMETRIC ENCRYPTION OF THE PRIVATE KEY [Chunk 1] To specify an asymmetric encryption key to be used to encrypt the server-generated private key, the client MUST include an AsymmetricDecryptKeyIdentifier attribute. Distribution of the key specified by the AsymmetricDecryptKeyIdentifier to the key generator and the client is outside the scope of this document. If the key identified is bound to an X.509 certificate, then the key MUST either explicitly support keyTransport or keyAgreement or its use MUST be unrestricted.

Distribution of the key specified by the AsymmetricDecryptKeyIdentifier to the key generator and the client is outside the scope of this document. If the key identified is bound to an X.509 certificate, then the key MUST either explicitly support keyTransport or keyAgreement or its use MUST be unrestricted.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 46: SERVER-SIDE KEY GENERATION RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If the request is successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code with a content-type of "multipart/mixed" consisting of two parts: one part is the private key data and the other part is the certificate data. [Chunk 2] The format in which the private key data part is returned is dependent on whether the private key is being returned with additional encryption on top of that provided by TLS. [Chunk 3] If additional encryption is not being employed, the private key data MUST be placed in an "application/pkcs8". An "application/pkcs8" part consists of the base64-encoded DER-encoded [X.690] PrivateKeyInfo with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 4] If additional encryption is being employed, the private key is placed inside of a CMS SignedData. The SignedData is signed by the party that generated the private key, which may or may not be the EST server or the EST CA. The SignedData is further protected by placing it inside of a CMS EnvelopedData, as described in Section 4 of [RFC5958]. The following list shows how the EncryptedData is used, depending on the type of protection key specified by the client. [Chunk 5] If the client specified a symmetric encryption key to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using the secret key identified in the request. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the key-encryption kekri key management technique [Chunk 6] . The values are as follows: version is set to 4, key-encryption key identifier (kekid) is set to the value of the DecryptKeyIdentifier from Section 4.4.1.1; keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request; and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 7] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key transport operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyTransRecipientInfo (ktri) key management technique [Chunk 8] . In KeyTransRecipientInfo, the RecipientIdentifier (rid) is either the subjectKeyIdentifier copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 or the server determines an associated issuerAndSerialNumber from the attribute; version is derived from the choice of rid [RFC5652], keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request, and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 9] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key agreement operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo (kari) key management technique [Chunk 10] . In the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo type, version, originator, and user keying material (ukm) are as in [RFC5652], and keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request. The recipient's key identifier is either copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 to subjectKeyIdentifier or the server determines an IssuerAndSerialNumber that corresponds to the value provided in the attribute. [Chunk 11] In all three additional encryption cases, the EnvelopedData is returned in the response as an "application/pkcs7-mime" part with an smime-type parameter of "server-generated-key" and a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64". [Chunk 12] The certificate data part is an "application/pkcs7-mime" and exactly matches the certificate response to /simpleenroll. [Chunk 13] When rejecting a request, the server MUST specify either an HTTP 4xx error or an HTTP 5xx error. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human-readable error message.
Reason: Negative tests for client provision_cert error handling; unrelated to server-side key-generation response. | The test generates a private key locally and calls est_client_provision_cert(), asserting the call returns EST_ERR_NONE. This demonstrates the happy-path success of certificate provisioning from the server (analogous to an HTTP 200 success). There is no assertion shown that verifies a Content-Type of "multipart/mixed" nor that the response body contains two MIME parts including a private-key part produced by the server (the test provides the private key to the API). Thus coverage is partial. | Client-side certificate/key loading and assertions; no assertions verifying server-side multipart/mixed response containing both private key and certificate parts. | This test reads raw key bytes and calls est_load_key with EST_FORMAT_PEM and asserts the key is parsed. This verifies format-specific parsing (PEM) but does not assert conditional behavior based on additional encryption beyond TLS. | Reads private key and certificate from files (PEM) and loads them; no assertions regarding application/pkcs8 MIME part formation or Content-Transfer-Encoding header values. | This test provisions a cert using a newly generated key but does not assert CMS SignedData or EnvelopedData packaging of the private key. | Calls est_client_provision_cert and asserts success; no inspection of PKCS7 EnvelopedData contents or RecipientInfo specifying KEKRI. | Cleanup/teardown code and comments; no verification of requirement behavior. | Creates client context, sets auth, generates a key and calls provisioning APIs; assertions check success of operations but do not inspect or assert values for version, kekid, keyEncryptionAlgorithm, or encryptedKey. | Sets auth (with cert/key), optionally enables hint, calls provision_cert and asserts return codes; assertions validate API success but do not check the detailed envelope fields required by the requirement. | This chunk shows cleanup and description of enroll flow but contains no assertions on EnvelopedData internals or recipient info. | Calls provision API and asserts success; no validation of how server protected the server-generated private key (EnvelopedData details). | This test contains cleanup and descriptive comments about an enroll sequence but no assertions that inspect EnvelopedData, symmetric key generation strength, or RecipientInfo fields. | Performs provisioning (est_client_provision_cert) and asserts return codes, but contains no checks verifying EnvelopedData encryption method, symmetric key generation or RecipientInfo KARI. | Cleanup and test description only; no assertions about content-type or exact matching of certificate data part.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 46: SERVER-SIDE KEY GENERATION RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If the request is successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code with a content-type of "multipart/mixed" consisting of two parts: one part is the private key data and the other part is the certificate data. [Chunk 2] The format in which the private key data part is returned is dependent on whether the private key is being returned with additional encryption on top of that provided by TLS. [Chunk 3] If additional encryption is not being employed, the private key data MUST be placed in an "application/pkcs8". An "application/pkcs8" part consists of the base64-encoded DER-encoded [X.690] PrivateKeyInfo with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 4] If additional encryption is being employed, the private key is placed inside of a CMS SignedData. The SignedData is signed by the party that generated the private key, which may or may not be the EST server or the EST CA. The SignedData is further protected by placing it inside of a CMS EnvelopedData, as described in Section 4 of [RFC5958]. The following list shows how the EncryptedData is used, depending on the type of protection key specified by the client. [Chunk 5] If the client specified a symmetric encryption key to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using the secret key identified in the request. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the key-encryption kekri key management technique [Chunk 6] . The values are as follows: version is set to 4, key-encryption key identifier (kekid) is set to the value of the DecryptKeyIdentifier from Section 4.4.1.1; keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request; and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 7] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key transport operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyTransRecipientInfo (ktri) key management technique [Chunk 8] . In KeyTransRecipientInfo, the RecipientIdentifier (rid) is either the subjectKeyIdentifier copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 or the server determines an associated issuerAndSerialNumber from the attribute; version is derived from the choice of rid [RFC5652], keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request, and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 9] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key agreement operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo (kari) key management technique [Chunk 10] . In the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo type, version, originator, and user keying material (ukm) are as in [RFC5652], and keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request. The recipient's key identifier is either copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 to subjectKeyIdentifier or the server determines an IssuerAndSerialNumber that corresponds to the value provided in the attribute. [Chunk 11] In all three additional encryption cases, the EnvelopedData is returned in the response as an "application/pkcs7-mime" part with an smime-type parameter of "server-generated-key" and a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64". [Chunk 12] The certificate data part is an "application/pkcs7-mime" and exactly matches the certificate response to /simpleenroll. [Chunk 13] When rejecting a request, the server MUST specify either an HTTP 4xx error or an HTTP 5xx error. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human-readable error message.

Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 46: SERVER-SIDE KEY GENERATION RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If the request is successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code with a content-type of "multipart/mixed" consisting of two parts: one part is the private key data and the other part is the certificate data. [Chunk 2] The format in which the private key data part is returned is dependent on whether the private key is being returned with additional encryption on top of that provided by TLS. [Chunk 3] If additional encryption is not being employed, the private key data MUST be placed in an "application/pkcs8". An "application/pkcs8" part consists of the base64-encoded DER-encoded [X.690] PrivateKeyInfo with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 4] If additional encryption is being employed, the private key is placed inside of a CMS SignedData. The SignedData is signed by the party that generated the private key, which may or may not be the EST server or the EST CA. The SignedData is further protected by placing it inside of a CMS EnvelopedData, as described in Section 4 of [RFC5958]. The following list shows how the EncryptedData is used, depending on the type of protection key specified by the client. [Chunk 5] If the client specified a symmetric encryption key to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using the secret key identified in the request. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the key-encryption kekri key management technique [Chunk 6] . In the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo type, version, originator, and user keying material (ukm) are as in [RFC5652], and keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request. The recipient's key identifier is either copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 to subjectKeyIdentifier or the server determines an IssuerAndSerialNumber that corresponds to the value provided in the attribute. [Chunk 11] In all three additional encryption cases, the EnvelopedData is returned in the response as an "application/pkcs7-mime" part with an smime-type parameter of "server-generated-key" and a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64". [Chunk 12] The certificate data part is an "application/pkcs7-mime" and exactly matches the certificate response to /simpleenroll. Description: [Chunk 0] REQUIREMENT 46: SERVER-SIDE KEY GENERATION RESPONSE [Chunk 1] If the request is successful, the server response MUST have an HTTP 200 response code with a content-type of "multipart/mixed" consisting of two parts: one part is the private key data and the other part is the certificate data. [Chunk 2] The format in which the private key data part is returned is dependent on whether the private key is being returned with additional encryption on top of that provided by TLS. [Chunk 3] If additional encryption is not being employed, the private key data MUST be placed in an "application/pkcs8". An "application/pkcs8" part consists of the base64-encoded DER-encoded [X.690] PrivateKeyInfo with a Content-Transfer-Encoding of "base64" [RFC2045]. [Chunk 4] If additional encryption is being employed, the private key is placed inside of a CMS SignedData. The SignedData is signed by the party that generated the private key, which may or may not be the EST server or the EST CA. The SignedData is further protected by placing it inside of a CMS EnvelopedData, as described in Section 4 of [RFC5958]. The following list shows how the EncryptedData is used, depending on the type of protection key specified by the client. [Chunk 5] If the client specified a symmetric encryption key to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using the secret key identified in the request. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the key-encryption kekri key management technique [Chunk 6] . The values are as follows: version is set to 4, key-encryption key identifier (kekid) is set to the value of the DecryptKeyIdentifier from Section 4.4.1.1; keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request; and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 7] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key transport operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyTransRecipientInfo (ktri) key management technique [Chunk 8] . In KeyTransRecipientInfo, the RecipientIdentifier (rid) is either the subjectKeyIdentifier copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 or the server determines an associated issuerAndSerialNumber from the attribute; version is derived from the choice of rid [RFC5652], keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request, and encryptedKey is the encrypted key. [Chunk 9] If the client specified an asymmetric encryption key suitable for key agreement operations to protect the server-generated private key, the EnvelopedData content is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric encryption key. The cryptographic strength of the symmetric encryption key SHOULD be equivalent to the client- specified asymmetric key. The EnvelopedData RecipientInfo field MUST indicate the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo (kari) key management technique [Chunk 10] . In the KeyAgreeRecipientInfo type, version, originator, and user keying material (ukm) are as in [RFC5652], and keyEncryptionAlgorithm is set to one of the key wrap algorithms that the client included in the SMIMECapabilities accompanying the request. The recipient's key identifier is either copied from the attribute defined in Section 4.4.1.2 to subjectKeyIdentifier or the server determines an IssuerAndSerialNumber that corresponds to the value provided in the attribute. [Chunk 11] In all three additional encryption cases, the EnvelopedData is returned in the response as an "application/pkcs7-mime" part with an smime-type parameter of "server-generated-key" and a Content- Transfer-Encoding of "base64". [Chunk 12] The certificate data part is an "application/pkcs7-mime" and exactly matches the certificate response to /simpleenroll. [Chunk 13] When rejecting a request, the server MUST specify either an HTTP 4xx error or an HTTP 5xx error. If the content-type is not set, the response data MUST be a plaintext human-readable error message.
